title: SC Bastia

infobox.value: *I Turchini* (The Blues),  *I Lioni di Furiani* (The Lions of [Furiani](/wiki/Furiani "Furiani")),[[1]](#cite_note-nickname-1)  *Le Sporting* (The Sporting),   *Les Bleus* (The Blues)

([Corsican](/wiki/Corsican_language "Corsican language"): *Sporting Club di Bastia*, commonly referred to as **SC Bastia** or simply **Bastia**; French: [[bastja]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-Jules78120-Bastia.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jules78120-Bastia.wav")) is a French professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Bastia](/wiki/Bastia "Bastia") on the island of [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica"). The club plays in [Ligue 2](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2"), the second tier of [French football](/wiki/Football_in_France "Football in France"), having won the [2020–21 Championnat National](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Championnat_National "2020–21 Championnat National").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The club plays its home matches at the [Stade Armand Cesari](/wiki/Stade_Armand_Cesari "Stade Armand Cesari") located within the city. SC Bastia is known for its strong association with [Corsican nationalism](/wiki/Corsican_nationalism "Corsican nationalism").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Association football club in Furiani, France

This article is about the football club from Bastia, Corsica, founded in 1905. For the club from the same city founded in 1920, see [CA Bastia](/wiki/CA_Bastia "CA Bastia"). For the club founded in 1920 from Biguglia, Corsica, see [ÉF Bastia](/wiki/%C3%89F_Bastia "ÉF Bastia"). For the Italian club from Bastia Umbra, see [A.C. Bastia 1924](/wiki/A.C._Bastia_1924 "A.C. Bastia 1924").

**Sporting Club Bastia** ([Corsican](/wiki/Corsican_language "Corsican language"): *Sporting Club di Bastia*, commonly referred to as **SC Bastia** or simply **Bastia**; French: [[bastja]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:LL-Q150_(fra)-Jules78120-Bastia.wav "File:LL-Q150 (fra)-Jules78120-Bastia.wav")) is a French professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Bastia](/wiki/Bastia "Bastia") on the island of [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica"). The club plays in [Ligue 2](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2"), the second tier of [French football](/wiki/Football_in_France "Football in France"), having won the [2020–21 Championnat National](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Championnat_National "2020–21 Championnat National").[[3]](#cite_note-3) The club plays its home matches at the [Stade Armand Cesari](/wiki/Stade_Armand_Cesari "Stade Armand Cesari") located within the city. SC Bastia is known for its strong association with [Corsican nationalism](/wiki/Corsican_nationalism "Corsican nationalism").[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Bastia's main historical success include reaching [the final](/wiki/1978_UEFA_Cup_Final "1978 UEFA Cup Final") of the [1977–78 edition](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_UEFA_Cup "1977–78 UEFA Cup") of the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup"). The team was defeated by Dutch club [PSV Eindhoven](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven") (0–0 at home, 0–3 away). Domestically, Bastia won the [second division](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2") of [French football](/wiki/Football_in_France "Football in France") in [1968](/wiki/1967%E2%80%9368_French_Division_2 "1967–68 French Division 2") and [2012](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Ligue_2 "2011–12 Ligue 2"), and the [Coupe de France](/wiki/Coupe_de_France "Coupe de France") in [1981](/wiki/1981_Coupe_de_France_Final "1981 Coupe de France Final"). During the club's infancy, it was league champions of the "Corsican League" 17 times. They are the local rivals of [Ajaccio](/wiki/AC_Ajaccio "AC Ajaccio") and contest the [Derby Corse](/wiki/Corsica_derby "Corsica derby").

The club has signed several famous players in its history, notably including [Dragan Džajić](/wiki/Dragan_D%C5%BEaji%C4%87 "Dragan Džajić"), [Claude Papi](/wiki/Claude_Papi "Claude Papi"), [Johnny Rep](/wiki/Johnny_Rep "Johnny Rep"), [Roger Milla](/wiki/Roger_Milla "Roger Milla"), [Michael Essien](/wiki/Michael_Essien "Michael Essien"), [Alex Song](/wiki/Alex_Song "Alex Song"), [Sébastien Squillaci](/wiki/S%C3%A9bastien_Squillaci "Sébastien Squillaci"), [Jérôme Rothen](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_Rothen "Jérôme Rothen"), [Antar Yahia](/wiki/Antar_Yahia "Antar Yahia") and [Florian Thauvin](/wiki/Florian_Thauvin "Florian Thauvin").

In 2017 the club was relegated to the [Championnat National 3](/wiki/Championnat_National_3 "Championnat National 3") due to financial irregularities and lost its professional licence. *I Turchini* regained professional status in 2021 following promotion to Ligue 2.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Genesis of the team and beginning of professional football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Genesis of the team and beginning of professional football")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/D%C3%A9but_du_SCB.jpg/250px-D%C3%A9but_du_SCB.jpg)](/wiki/File:D%C3%A9but_du_SCB.jpg)

Sporting Club de Bastia in staff (1905)

Sporting Club de Bastia was founded in 1905 by a Swiss named Hans Ruesch. He taught German in high school in [Bastia](/wiki/Bastia "Bastia"). The first president of Bastia was Emile Brandizi. The Corsican club celebrated its debut on the Place d'Armes of Bastia, in the light of a single gas burner.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The club began its professional journey in 1965, in [Division 2](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2"). After three successful seasons, it was crowned Champion of France's Second Division in [1968](/wiki/1967-68_French_Division_2 "1967-68 French Division 2"), joining the elite. The first season was difficult, but the club still maintained its place. Thus began a decade widely considered the finest in club history. [In 1972](/wiki/1971-72_French_Division_1 "1971-72 French Division 1"), the club reached, for the first time, the [final](/wiki/1972_Coupe_de_France_Final "1972 Coupe de France Final") of the [Coupe de France](/wiki/Coupe_de_France "Coupe de France") against [Olympique de Marseille](/wiki/Olympique_de_Marseille "Olympique de Marseille") (losing 2–1), getting it its first qualification in the [European Cup Winners' Cup](/wiki/European_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "European Cup Winners' Cup"), which led to [elimination](/wiki/1972-73_European_Cup_Winners%27_Cup "1972-73 European Cup Winners' Cup") against the excellent team of [Atlético Madrid](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Madrid "Atlético Madrid").

### UEFA Cup finalist in 1978 and Cup victory in France in 1981

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: UEFA Cup finalist in 1978 and Cup victory in France in 1981")]

In 1977, Bastia [finished third](/wiki/1976-77_French_Division_1 "1976-77 French Division 1") in the [Division 1](/wiki/Ligue_1 "Ligue 1") with the best offence in the league, with magnificent [Dragan Džajić](/wiki/Dragan_D%C5%BEaji%C4%87 "Dragan Džajić") as a left winger
[[6]](#cite_note-6) and [qualified for](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_UEFA_Cup "1977–78 UEFA Cup") the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup").

This is the beginning of the team built around playmaker [Claude Papi](/wiki/Claude_Papi "Claude Papi"), which was composed of many talented players such as [Johnny Rep](/wiki/Johnny_Rep "Johnny Rep") of the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands_national_football_team "Netherlands national football team"); [Jean-François Larios](/wiki/Jean-Fran%C3%A7ois_Larios "Jean-François Larios"), a [midfielder](/wiki/Midfielder_(association_football) "Midfielder (association football)") and [French international](/wiki/France_national_football_team "France national football team"); and [Charles Orlanducci](/wiki/Charles_Orlanducci "Charles Orlanducci"), the solid libero nicknamed the *"Lion of Vescovato"*.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Claude_papi.jpg/250px-Claude_papi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Claude_papi.jpg)

Claude Papi, 1978.

The team eliminated successively [Sporting Lisbon](/wiki/Sporting_Clube_de_Portugal "Sporting Clube de Portugal"), [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C."), [Torino](/wiki/Torino_FC "Torino FC"), [Carl Zeiss Jena](/wiki/FC_Carl_Zeiss_Jena "FC Carl Zeiss Jena") and [Grasshoppers Zurich](/wiki/Grasshoppers_Zurich "Grasshoppers Zurich") before a [final](/wiki/1978_UEFA_Cup_Final "1978 UEFA Cup Final") defeat to [PSV Eindhoven](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven") (0–0, 0–3). Of all the victories, the 3–2 win in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin") made the most lasting impression, the *"Toro"* being undefeated for two seasons on their home pitch. It is also during this match that Bastia marks the best goal of that time, a volley from 22 meters by Jean-François Larios.

The final would, however, end with misfortune. First, with a first leg played at [Furiani](/wiki/Furiani "Furiani") on an unplayable pitch,[[7]](#cite_note-7) heavy rain having fallen on Corsica on 26 April 1978. Unfortunately, the [Yugoslav](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia") referee postponed the meeting because of the proximity to World Cup in [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina") that was scheduled a few weeks later. Despite Bastia's dominance, the meeting ended with a goalless draw, 0–0.
The final return, 9 May, lasted only 24 minutes. This is the time it took [PSV Eindhoven](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven") to score the first goal, followed by two more late in the game (3–0). The townsfolk will attribute the defeat of Bastia to fatigue accumulated in the league (3 games in 6 days in the days before the final return) and also the rain-soaked pitch in the first leg at Furiani.

Bastia then realised the greatest moment in Corsican sport (see the movie [Forza Bastia](/wiki/Forza_Bastia "Forza Bastia") of [Jacques Tati](/wiki/Jacques_Tati "Jacques Tati")). The ECBC club, from a town of only 40,000 souls, had done more than challenge the major capitals of [European football](/wiki/UEFA "UEFA"): it had allowed the whole of Corsica to meet, at a time when the nationalist movement was born, three years after the episode of Bastia, in 1975.

Three years after that [final](/wiki/1981_Coupe_de_France_Final "1981 Coupe de France Final"), the Bastia won its first trophy with the [Coupe de France](/wiki/Coupe_de_France "Coupe de France") [1980–81](/wiki/1980-81_Coupe_de_France "1980-81 Coupe de France"). This was a prestigious victory for the Corsican club facing [St. Etienne](/wiki/AS_Saint-Etienne "AS Saint-Etienne") of [Michel Platini](/wiki/Michel_Platini "Michel Platini"). The final was played at [Parc des Princes](/wiki/Parc_des_Princes "Parc des Princes") in front of more than 46,000 spectators, including the newly elected President of the Republic, [François Mitterrand](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Mitterrand "François Mitterrand").

### Descent in Division 2 and Furiani disaster

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Descent in Division 2 and Furiani disaster")]

See also: [Armand Cesari Stadium disaster](/wiki/Armand_Cesari_Stadium_disaster "Armand Cesari Stadium disaster")

After 18 years in the elite, the club went down to the second division at the end of the [1985–86 season](/wiki/1985-86_French_Division_1 "1985-86 French Division 1"), and stayed for eight years. This era is marked by the catastrophe of Furiani: In the [1991–92 season](/wiki/1991-92_Coupe_de_France "1991-92 Coupe de France"), the club reached the semi-finals of the Coupe de France. The match was played at [Stade Armand Cesari](/wiki/Stade_Armand_Cesari "Stade Armand Cesari"), or *"Furiani Stadium"*, against Olympique de Marseille which dominated the [Championship of France](/wiki/List_of_French_football_champions "List of French football champions"). The enthusiasm was such that was decided to hastily construct a temporary stand of 10,000 seats. The upper part of the platform collapsed a few minutes before kick-off killing 18 and injuring 2,300.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Alexander_Song.jpg/120px-Alexander_Song.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alexander_Song.jpg)

Alex Song, made his professional debut in 2004 for Bastia.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Michael_Essien_4633.jpg/120px-Michael_Essien_4633.jpg)](/wiki/File:Michael_Essien_4633.jpg)

Michael Essien, made his professional debut in 2000 for Bastia.

### Back in Ligue 1 and in the European Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Back in Ligue 1 and in the European Cup")]

The club returned to the elite for the [1994–95](/wiki/1994-95_French_Division_2 "1994-95 French Division 2") season and that year reached the [final](/wiki/1995_Coupe_de_la_Ligue_Final "1995 Coupe de la Ligue Final") of the [League Cup](/wiki/Coupe_de_la_Ligue "Coupe de la Ligue"). This era was marked by the work of [Frédéric Antonetti](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Antonetti "Frédéric Antonetti"), who coached the club between 1990 and 1994 and oversaw the development of new talents ([Morlaye Soumah](/wiki/Morlaye_Soumah "Morlaye Soumah"), [Laurent Casanova](/w/index.php?title=Laurent_Casanova_(footballer)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Laurent Casanova (footballer) (page does not exist)"), and [Cyril Rool](/wiki/Cyril_Rool "Cyril Rool")).

Antonetti coached the first team from 1994 to 2001 (with an interlude in [1998–99](/wiki/1998-99_French_Division_1 "1998-99 French Division 1")) and was involved in the recruitment of players such as [Lubomir Moravcik](/wiki/Lubomir_Moravcik "Lubomir Moravcik"), [Pierre-Yves André](/wiki/Pierre-Yves_Andr%C3%A9 "Pierre-Yves André"), [Frédéric Née](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_N%C3%A9e "Frédéric Née"), [Franck Jurietti](/wiki/Franck_Jurietti "Franck Jurietti"), and [Anto Drobnjak](/wiki/Anto_Drobnjak "Anto Drobnjak"), the latter of whom was the club's top scorer in his third season. At the end of the [1995–96 season](/wiki/1995-96_French_Division_1 "1995-96 French Division 1"), Drobnjak was also second in the Championship scoring charts with 20 goals, one goal behind top scorer [Sonny Anderson](/wiki/Sonny_Anderson "Sonny Anderson").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

In the [1996–97 season](/wiki/1996-97_French_Division_1 "1996-97 French Division 1") the club finished in 7th place in Ligue 1, just 3 points off bronze position, thus qualifying for the [Intertoto Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "UEFA Intertoto Cup"). The club won [the Intertoto Cup](/wiki/1997_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "1997 UEFA Intertoto Cup"), and qualified for the UEFA Cup for the [1997–98](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_UEFA_Cup "1997–98 UEFA Cup") season. Bastia eliminated [Benfica](/wiki/S.L._Benfica "S.L. Benfica") in the round of 32 before falling to [Steaua Bucharest](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București") in the next round. This defeat left a bitter taste due to the domination Bastia had in both matches.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The performance of Bastia in the league in the following years allowed it to qualify two more times for the Intertoto Cup, in [1998](/wiki/1998_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "1998 UEFA Intertoto Cup") and [2001](/wiki/2001_UEFA_Intertoto_Cup "2001 UEFA Intertoto Cup"), but failed to re-qualify for the UEFA Cup.

After the departure of Antonetti in 2001, the club would not finish in the top ten again. They did reach the [final](/wiki/2002_Coupe_de_France_Final "2002 Coupe de France Final") of the Coupe de France in [2001–02](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Coupe_de_France "2001–02 Coupe de France"); in their midfield was [Michael Essien](/wiki/Michael_Essien "Michael Essien"), who would go on to play for [Lyon](/wiki/Olympique_Lyonnais "Olympique Lyonnais") and [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Another player groomed by Bastia was [defender](/wiki/Defender_(association_football) "Defender (association football)") [Alex Song](/wiki/Alex_Song "Alex Song"), who later played for [Arsenal](/wiki/Arsenal_F.C. "Arsenal F.C.") and [Barcelona](/wiki/FC_Barcelona "FC Barcelona").[[12]](#cite_note-Arsenal_take_Song_on_season_loan-12)

Between 2002 and 2005, Bastia was managed by [Robert Nouzaret](/wiki/Robert_Nouzaret "Robert Nouzaret"), [Gerard Gili](/wiki/Gerard_Gili "Gerard Gili"), [François Ciccolini](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Ciccolini "François Ciccolini"), and lastly the duo of [Michel Padovani](/wiki/Michel_Padovani "Michel Padovani") and [Eric Durand](/wiki/Eric_Durand "Eric Durand"). Each failed to take the team to the top ten, the final league placing actually falling each year (see Section championship history), despite the first team featuring the likes of [Tony Vairelles](/wiki/Tony_Vairelles "Tony Vairelles"), [Florian Maurice](/wiki/Florian_Maurice "Florian Maurice"), [Franck Silvestre](/wiki/Franck_Silvestre "Franck Silvestre"), [Lilian Laslandes](/wiki/Lilian_Laslandes "Lilian Laslandes") (all internationals) and [Cyril Jeunechamp](/wiki/Cyril_Jeunechamp "Cyril Jeunechamp").

In the winter break of the [2004–05 season](/wiki/2004%E2%80%9305_Ligue_1 "2004–05 Ligue 1"), the club fell into the relegation zone. Bastia recruited [Christian Karembeu](/wiki/Christian_Karembeu "Christian Karembeu"), member of the [1998](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup") World Cup-winning France team, but Bastia would still be relegated [to Ligue 2](/wiki/2005%E2%80%9306_Ligue_2 "2005–06 Ligue 2") at the end of the season after 11 consecutive years in Ligue 1.

### The Descent Below

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: The Descent Below")]

In 2005, the club was relegated to [Ligue 2](/wiki/Ligue_2 "Ligue 2"). Five years later, Bastia was in serious danger of descent into the [Championnat National](/wiki/Championnat_National "Championnat National"). Bastia was officially relegated to the National on 7 May 2010, following a draw (0–0) with [Tours](/wiki/Tours_FC "Tours FC") at the 37th matchday [of Ligue 2](/wiki/2009%E2%80%9310_Ligue_2 "2009–10 Ligue 2").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

On 6 July 2010, the club was administratively relegated to [Championnat de France amateur](/wiki/Championnat_de_France_amateur "Championnat de France amateur") by the [Direction Nationale du Contrôle de Gestion](/wiki/Direction_Nationale_du_Contr%C3%B4le_de_Gestion "Direction Nationale du Contrôle de Gestion") (DNCG). The club actually had a deficit of €1.2 million, which was filled by grants from local governments ([Territorial Community of Corsica](/w/index.php?title=Territorial_Community_of_Corsica&action=edit&redlink=1 "Territorial Community of Corsica (page does not exist)"), the [General Council of Haute-Corse](/w/index.php?title=General_Council_of_Haute-Corse&action=edit&redlink=1 "General Council of Haute-Corse (page does not exist)")).[[14]](#cite_note-14) On 23 July 2010, the Federal Council of the [French Football Federation](/wiki/French_Football_Federation "French Football Federation") authorised Bastia to play in the [2010–11 season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_Championnat_National "2010–11 Championnat National"), as requested by the [National Olympic Committee and French sports](/wiki/French_Olympic_Committee "French Olympic Committee") (CNOSF) after the DNCG had refused.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Despite this disrupted pre-season, Bastia performed well in the transfer window, with no fewer than six rookies. As for departures, there is mainly the transfer of [Florent Ghisolfi](/wiki/Florent_Ghisolfi "Florent Ghisolfi") ([Reims](/wiki/Stade_de_Reims "Stade de Reims")) and [Christophe Gaffory](/wiki/Christophe_Gaffory "Christophe Gaffory") ([Vannes](/wiki/Vannes_OC "Vannes OC")) as well as Pierre-Yves André who decided to end his career.[[16]](#cite_note-bastia_2010-11_squad-16)

### The rise in Ligue 2

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: The rise in Ligue 2")]

See also: [2010–11 SC Bastia season](/wiki/2010%E2%80%9311_SC_Bastia_season "2010–11 SC Bastia season")

[Faruk Hadžibegić](/wiki/Faruk_Had%C5%BEibegi%C4%87 "Faruk Hadžibegić") was fired from the managers' post after poor results, and the job then passed to [Frédéric Hantz](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Hantz "Frédéric Hantz").[[17]](#cite_note-17) On 22 April 2011, Bastia officially earned its [place in Ligue 2](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_Ligue_2 "2011–12 Ligue 2") after a game against [Frejus-Saint Raphael](/wiki/%C3%89toile_Fr%C3%A9jus_Saint-Rapha%C3%ABl "Étoile Fréjus Saint-Raphaël").[[18]](#cite_note-18) No fewer than 500 townsfolk had made the trip. On 7 May 2011, Bastia become the National champion, following a victory over [Créteil](/wiki/US_Cr%C3%A9teil-Lusitanos "US Créteil-Lusitanos") by 2 goals to one, with Bastia finishing with a record tally of 91 points and unbeaten at home throughout the campaign. Sporting was leading at the half, but equalised by [David Suarez](/wiki/David_Suarez_(footballer) "David Suarez (footballer)"), then [Idrissa Sylla](/wiki/Idrissa_Sylla "Idrissa Sylla") allowed Bastia to take the lead in the 92nd minute in a crazy atmosphere.[[19]](#cite_note-19) At the end of the match, the pitch at Armand Cesari was invaded by Bastia fans, happy to celebrate with their players and their coach, Frédéric Hantz, this new title.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/Rothen.jpg/250px-Rothen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rothen.jpg)

Jérôme Rothen won Ligue 2's best player award in 2011–12, the same season Bastia were named team of the year and earned promotion to Ligue 1.

### Return to Ligue 1

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Return to Ligue 1")]

See also: [2011–12 SC Bastia season](/wiki/2011%E2%80%9312_SC_Bastia_season "2011–12 SC Bastia season")

Bastia, newly promoted from the National, welcomed [Jérôme Rothen](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_Rothen "Jérôme Rothen"), [Toifilou Maoulida](/wiki/Toifilou_Maoulida "Toifilou Maoulida"), [François Marque](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Marque "François Marque"), [Ludovic Genest](/wiki/Ludovic_Genest "Ludovic Genest") and [Florian Thauvin](/wiki/Florian_Thauvin "Florian Thauvin") into the club.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Bastia started off on a good note, falling off slightly in the autumn. From early February until the beginning of April, Bastia did not lose a single match.[[21]](#cite_note-21) On 23 April 2012, in a full Stadium Armand Cesari, Bastia virtually secured their place amongst the elite by winning against [Châteauroux](/wiki/LB_Ch%C3%A2teauroux "LB Châteauroux") (2–1).[[22]](#cite_note-22) On 1 May 2012, Bastia became champion of Ligue 2,[[23]](#cite_note-23) 44 years after its first and only league title, with their victory over [Metz](/wiki/FC_Metz "FC Metz") at Armand Cesari. On 11 May 2012, Bastia won its last game of the season at home 2–1 against [Nantes](/wiki/FC_Nantes "FC Nantes") thanks to goals from [Jérôme Rothen](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_Rothen "Jérôme Rothen") and David Suarez.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The club was also on a 2-year run of being undefeated at home. Bastia became part of the very exclusive club of teams undefeated at home in Europe. Several players played their last game against Nantes in the colours of Bastia, including David Suarez and [Jacques-Désiré Périatambée](/wiki/Jacques-D%C3%A9sir%C3%A9_P%C3%A9riatamb%C3%A9e "Jacques-Désiré Périatambée").

Bastia won all the trophies [UNFP](/wiki/Troph%C3%A9es_UNFP_du_football "Trophées UNFP du football") for Ligue 2: [Jérôme Rothen](/wiki/J%C3%A9r%C3%B4me_Rothen "Jérôme Rothen"), best player; [Macedo Novaes](/wiki/Macedo_Novaes "Macedo Novaes"), best goalkeeper; and [Frédéric Hantz](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Hantz "Frédéric Hantz"), best coach, who placed five players in the team line-up (Macedo Novaes, [Féthi Harek](/wiki/F%C3%A9thi_Harek "Féthi Harek"), [Wahbi Khazri](/wiki/Wahbi_Khazri "Wahbi Khazri"), [Sadio Diallo](/wiki/Sadio_Diallo "Sadio Diallo") and Jérôme Rothen).[[25]](#cite_note-Rothen_en_iyi_oyuncu-25)

In the [2016–17 Ligue 1](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_Ligue_1 "2016–17 Ligue 1") season, after four seasons in the top division, Bastia finished bottom of the Ligue 1 table and were relegated to [Ligue 2](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Ligue_2 "2017–18 Ligue 2").

### Sharp fall, financial troubles and climb back to professional football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Sharp fall, financial troubles and climb back to professional football")]

On 22 June 2017, Bastia were relegated again to [Championnat National](/wiki/Championnat_National "Championnat National") after the [DNCG](/wiki/DNCG "DNCG") had recommended a further demotion for the club. Bastia were the subject of an audit on their books which resulted in yet another demotion for failing to guarantee they had the finances to compete in Ligue 2. The DNCG released a statement on the same day stating “Following its audit today before the DNCG, Sporting Club Bastia has been given notice of a provisional relegation". In August 2017 following bankruptcy proceedings, the clubs professional section was liquidated. The club was taken over by local entrepreneurs Claude Ferrandi (Ferrandi Group) and Pierre-Noël Luiggi ([Oscaro group](/wiki/Oscaro "Oscaro")). Following the takeover, the club restarted with its prior reserve team in [Championnat National 3](/wiki/Championnat_National_3 "Championnat National 3").[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) After a difficult first season in National 3, the club would embark on a series of back-to-back promotions, starting with promotion to the [Championnat National 2](/wiki/Championnat_National_2 "Championnat National 2") in the 2018–19 season,[[28]](#cite_note-28) and then to the [Championnat National](/wiki/Championnat_National "Championnat National") in the 2019–20 season, by being top of the National 2 Group A table when the season was curtailed due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[29]](#cite_note-29) Bastia achieved their third successive promotion after being crowned champion of the [2020–21 Championnat National](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Championnat_National "2020–21 Championnat National").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### Creation of a cooperative structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Creation of a cooperative structure")]

At the beginning of the 2019-2020 [Championnat National 2](/wiki/Championnat_National_2 "Championnat National 2"), the club announced[[31]](#cite_note-31) the creation of a cooperative structure, under the form of a [Société coopérative d'intérêt collectif](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soci%C3%A9t%C3%A9_coop%C3%A9rative_d%27int%C3%A9r%C3%AAt_collectif "fr:Société coopérative d'intérêt collectif"), which allows anyone identifying in different types of supporter categories to invest and hold shares in the club. The voting rights structure and board is divided into four colleges:

* Founders (Ferrandi and Luiggi families) - 38% voting rights and 4 board seats
* Economic actors (businesses and organizations) - 22% voting rights and 2 board seats
* Supporters - 20% voting rights and 2 board seats
* Employees and prior employees (players, staff and administrative personnel) - 10% voting rights and 1 board seat
* Public entities (Regional and municipal authorities) - 10% voting rights
* SC Bastia Association - 1 board seat

This structure allows the club to involve any type of supporter into the clubs decision-making, in a transparent structure.

### Return to professional football

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Return to professional football")]

SC Bastia returned to professional football for the first time since its bankruptcy in 2017, playing in the [2021–22 Ligue 2](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Ligue_2 "2021–22 Ligue 2"). After a difficult start to the season which would result in the sacking[[32]](#cite_note-32) of manager [Mathieu Chabert](/wiki/Mathieu_Chabert "Mathieu Chabert") on September 22, following a 2–1 defeat away to Valenciennes, with Bastia 18th in the table, just before the derby against [AC Ajaccio](/wiki/AC_Ajaccio "AC Ajaccio"). The club would then announce the hiring of [Régis Brouard](/wiki/R%C3%A9gis_Brouard "Régis Brouard") on October 2, 2021.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The club would go on to a comfortable mid-table finish in 12th.

The [following season](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Ligue_2 "2022–23 Ligue 2"), the club would go on to an up-and-down first half of season, before an extremely strong second half that would see Regis Brouard guide the club to a 4th-place finish, having for a long time looked towards promotion in a three-way fight with Bordeaux and Metz. During the summer break, expectations were high and the fans wanted to see the club fight for promotion. The club would go on to have a difficult first half of the season, being 15th at the halfway point. In a repeat of the previous sacking, coach Regis Brouard would be sacked following a 3–1 defeat away to Valenciennes and just before the derby against Ajaccio on January 29.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Reserve team coach [Michel Moretti](/wiki/Michel_Moretti "Michel Moretti") and assistant coach [Lillian Laslandes](/wiki/Lilian_Laslandes "Lilian Laslandes") were brought on as caretaker coaches until the end of the season, guiding the team to a 13th-place finish.

The club announced the arrival and return of [Frédéric Antonetti](/wiki/Fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric_Antonetti "Frédéric Antonetti") as Technical Director[[35]](#cite_note-35) on March 26, 2024, with the responsibility of overseeing the sporting policy of the first team as well as the youth and reserve teams. Benoit Tavenot[[36]](#cite_note-36) would then be named first team coach on June 5, also returning to SC Bastia for the [2024–25 Ligue 2](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Ligue_2 "2024–25 Ligue 2") season.

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Stadium")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Stade_Armand_Cesari_2012.png/500px-Stade_Armand_Cesari_2012.png)](/wiki/File:Stade_Armand_Cesari_2012.png)

Stade Armand Cesari.

Main article: [Stade Armand Cesari](/wiki/Stade_Armand_Cesari "Stade Armand Cesari")

Stade Armand Cesari, also known as Stade Furiani, is the main football stadium in Corsica. It is located in Furiani, and is used by SC Bastia. In 1992, the stadium hosted the [semi-finals](/wiki/1991-92_Coupe_de_France "1991-92 Coupe de France") of the [Coupe de France](/wiki/Coupe_de_France "Coupe de France") during which a temporary grandstand collapsed, killing 18 people and injuring nearly 3,000.[[37]](#cite_note-stade_armand_cesari-37)

Totally obsolete and even dangerous (barbed wire around the ground, dilapidated stands), the stadium hosted the [1978 UEFA Cup Final](/wiki/1978_UEFA_Cup_Final "1978 UEFA Cup Final"). The stadium's capacity was then less than 12,000 seats, in precarious conditions, heavy rain having fallen on Corsica that day, turning the ground into a quagmire, which handicapped the outcome of this decisive match (0–0).[[37]](#cite_note-stade_armand_cesari-37) The crowd of 15,000 spectators announced appears exaggerated, but given the fervor not hesitate to stand up, packed tightly, to attend the game. The record attendance at the stadium was set on 1 September 2012, when 15,505 people saw Bastia lose against [St. Etienne](/wiki/AS_Saint-Etienne "AS Saint-Etienne") (0–3) in a league match.[[37]](#cite_note-stade_armand_cesari-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Behind it the following record was set in 1978, when 15,000 people saw Bastia draw against [PSV Eindhoven](/wiki/PSV_Eindhoven "PSV Eindhoven") (0–0, 1978) in the [UEFA Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Cup "UEFA Cup") final matches.[[37]](#cite_note-stade_armand_cesari-37)

"Bastia" is the club of the city. 2- To recall the historic jersey from 1978, the shield has a moor's head, from the Testa Mora Flag. 3- It also reappeared as in the 70s and the heyday of the club. 4- The dominant color is blue. ## Supporters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=SC_Bastia&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Supporters")]

Bastia has a large number of supporters among [Corsicans](/wiki/Corsicans "Corsicans"), and their supporters frequently display elements of [Corsican nationalism](/wiki/Corsican_nationalism "Corsican nationalism"), such as the frequent use of the [local language](/wiki/Corsican_language "Corsican language") and [symbols](/wiki/Flag_of_Corsica "Flag of Corsica"),[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41) and support for the [island's independence](/wiki/Corsican_independence "Corsican independence").[[42]](#cite_note-42) The fans are known as the *Turchini*, meaning "Blues" in Corsican.

The fans have a rivalry with most mainland supporters, however, their most fierce rivals are [Nice](/wiki/OGC_Nice "OGC Nice") with whom they contest the [Derby de la Mediterranée](/wiki/Derby_de_la_Mediterran%C3%A9e "Derby de la Mediterranée"), although the derby can also refer to rivalries with [Marseille](/wiki/Olympique_Marseille "Olympique Marseille") and [Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco "AS Monaco"). They also have a rivalry with [Parisian](/wiki/Paris "Paris") club [PSG](/wiki/Paris_Saint-Germain_F.C. "Paris Saint-Germain F.C.")[[43]](#cite_note-43) due to political tensions between the [capital](/wiki/Capital_city "Capital city") and Corsica.

The other large rivalry is the [Corsican derby](/wiki/Derby_Corse "Derby Corse") with fellow islanders [AC Ajaccio](/wiki/AC_Ajaccio "AC Ajaccio"), and to a lesser extent [Gazélec Ajaccio](/wiki/Gaz%C3%A9lec_Ajaccio "Gazélec Ajaccio"), with whom they compete over the dominance of the island.[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Since 2004, the Bastia ultras have maintained a relationship of friendship and respect with the Nuova Guardia, the historic [Torres](/wiki/SEF_Torres_1903 "SEF Torres 1903") ultras group.
